1	"""Per-cycle run-log artefact (US-C / ABA-196).
2	
3	Each ``drain-cycle`` invocation produces a single JSON file at
4	``~/.drain-cycle/runs/<cycle-id>-<run-timestamp>.json`` capturing one
5	entry per attempted issue. The filename embeds the run-start timestamp
6	(UTC, ``%Y%m%dT%H%M%S%fZ``) so re-running ``drain-cycle`` against the
7	same cycle writes a new file instead of clobbering the prior one
8	(ABA-230). Downstream consumers glob the directory and group by
9	``cycle_id`` (carried inside each file).
10	
11	* KR1 (completion %) is computed from ``entries[].final_linear_state``
12	  merged across every file sharing the same ``cycle_id``.
13	* US-D (cycle-drain self-grading) reads every file across cycles.
14	* The kill condition (KR1 < 50%) merges per-cycle then thresholds.
15	
16	Schema:
17	
18	::
19	
20	    {
21	      "cycle_id":               "<linear-cycle-uuid>",
22	      "cycle_duration_seconds": <float>,
23	      "entries":                [
24	        {
25	          "issue_identifier":   "ABA-NNN",
26	          "started_at":         "<iso-8601 UTC>",
27	          "finished_at":        "<iso-8601 UTC>",
28	          "exit_code":          <int>,
29	          "final_linear_state": "Done" | "Todo" | ...,
30	          "worktree_path":      "<absolute path>",
31	          "halt_reason":        null | "<orchestrator stderr halt line>",
32	        },
33	        ...
34	      ],
35	    }
36	
37	``halt_reason`` is ``null`` on every Done entry (the agent flipped state
38	as required). On the orchestrator's halt entry it is the exact string
39	also written to stderr — both surfaces are produced by the same
40	``_halt_message`` helper in ``orchestrator.py`` so the on-disk and
41	terminal values cannot drift (US-B / ABA-213). Non-last entries are
42	``null`` by construction: the orchestrator returns on first halt, so
43	anything before the halt is a Done entry.
44	
45	``cycle_duration_seconds`` is computed automatically on every persist as
46	``max(finished_at) - min(started_at)`` across ``entries`` (``0.0`` when
47	empty). This is the KR2 grading proxy — "how long was the agent doing
48	execution for me unattended" stands in for "operator hands-off time" —
49	and replaces the originally specified ``time_spent`` block, which
50	required manual self-report at cycle close and was never going to be
51	filled in.
52	
53	Persistence is incremental: ``append_entry`` re-serialises the whole dict
54	on every call, so a mid-run crash still leaves a well-formed file with
55	every issue attempted so far. Cycle scale (≤ 15 issues) makes the cost
56	irrelevant.
57	"""
58	from __future__ import annotations
59	
60	import json
61	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
62	from datetime import datetime, timezone
63	from pathlib import Path
64	from typing import Any
65	
66	
67	def runs_dir() -> Path:
68	    """Return the directory where per-cycle run logs are written.
69	
70	    Resolved on every call (not module-import time) so tests can redirect
71	    via ``monkeypatch.setenv("HOME", ...)`` after the module is imported.
72	    """
73	    return Path.home() / ".drain-cycle" / "runs"
74	
75	
76	@dataclass
77	class RunLog:
78	    """Per-cycle run-log file. Construct once at orchestrator start.
79	
80	    Writing the initial ``{cycle_id, cycle_duration_seconds: 0.0,
81	    entries: []}`` shell in ``__post_init__`` means that even a zero-issue
82	    cycle (or a crash before the first ``append_entry``) leaves the
83	    artefact on disk, which is what US-D / kill-condition tooling needs
84	    to distinguish "drained nothing" from "never ran".
85	
86	    The filename embeds a UTC run-start timestamp with microsecond
87	    resolution, so two ``RunLog`` instances on the same ``cycle_id``
88	    (re-running ``drain-cycle`` after a halt) write to two separate
89	    files instead of one clobbering the other (ABA-230).
90	    """
91	
92	    cycle_id: str
93	    path: Path = field(init=False)
94	    entries: list[dict[str, Any]] = field(default_factory=list, init=False)
95	
96	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
97	        directory = runs_dir()
98	        directory.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
99	        run_started_at = datetime.now(tz=timezone.utc)
100	        timestamp = run_started_at.strftime("%Y%m%dT%H%M%S%fZ")
101	        self.path = directory / f"{self.cycle_id}-{timestamp}.json"
102	        self._persist()
103	
104	    def append_entry(
105	        self,
106	        *,
107	        issue_identifier: str,
108	        started_at: str,
109	        finished_at: str,
110	        exit_code: int,
111	        final_linear_state: str,
112	        worktree_path: str,
113	        halt_reason: str | None = None,
114	    ) -> None:
115	        self.entries.append(
116	            {
117	                "issue_identifier": issue_identifier,
118	                "started_at": started_at,
119	                "finished_at": finished_at,
120	                "exit_code": exit_code,
121	                "final_linear_state": final_linear_state,
122	                "worktree_path": worktree_path,
123	                "halt_reason": halt_reason,
124	            }
125	        )
126	        self._persist()
127	
128	    def _persist(self) -> None:
129	        payload = {
130	            "cycle_id": self.cycle_id,
131	            "cycle_duration_seconds": self._cycle_duration_seconds(),
132	            "entries": self.entries,
133	        }
134	        self.path.write_text(json.dumps(payload, indent=2) + "\n")
135	
136	    def _cycle_duration_seconds(self) -> float:
137	        if not self.entries:
138	            return 0.0
139	        starts = [datetime.fromisoformat(e["started_at"]) for e in self.entries]
140	        finishes = [datetime.fromisoformat(e["finished_at"]) for e in self.entries]
141	        return (max(finishes) - min(starts)).total_seconds()
142

file.content:
"""Per-cycle run-log artefact (US-C / ABA-196).

Each ``drain-cycle`` invocation produces a single JSON file at
``~/.drain-cycle/runs/<cycle-id>-<run-timestamp>.json`` capturing one
entry per attempted issue. The filename embeds the run-start timestamp
(UTC, ``%Y%m%dT%H%M%S%fZ``) so re-running ``drain-cycle`` against the
same cycle writes a new file instead of clobbering the prior one
(ABA-230). Downstream consumers glob the directory and group by
``cycle_id`` (carried inside each file).

* KR1 (completion %) is computed from ``entries[].final_linear_state``
  merged across every file sharing the same ``cycle_id``.
* US-D (cycle-drain self-grading) reads every file across cycles.
* The kill condition (KR1 < 50%) merges per-cycle then thresholds.

Schema:

::

    {
      "cycle_id":               "<linear-cycle-uuid>",
      "cycle_duration_seconds": <float>,
      "entries":                [
        {
          "issue_identifier":   "ABA-NNN",
          "started_at":         "<iso-8601 UTC>",
          "finished_at":        "<iso-8601 UTC>",
          "exit_code":          <int>,
          "final_linear_state": "Done" | "Todo" | ...,
          "worktree_path":      "<absolute path>",
          "halt_reason":        null | "<orchestrator stderr halt line>",
        },
        ...
      ],
    }

``halt_reason`` is ``null`` on every Done entry (the agent flipped state
as required). On the orchestrator's halt entry it is the exact string
also written to stderr — both surfaces are produced by the same
``_halt_message`` helper in ``orchestrator.py`` so the on-disk and
terminal values cannot drift (US-B / ABA-213). Non-last entries are
``null`` by construction: the orchestrator returns on first halt, so
anything before the halt is a Done entry.

``cycle_duration_seconds`` is computed automatically on every persist as
``max(finished_at) - min(started_at)`` across ``entries`` (``0.0`` when
empty). This is the KR2 grading proxy — "how long was the agent doing
execution for me unattended" stands in for "operator hands-off time" —
and replaces the originally specified ``time_spent`` block, which
required manual self-report at cycle close and was never going to be
filled in.

Persistence is incremental: ``append_entry`` re-serialises the whole dict
on every call, so a mid-run crash still leaves a well-formed file with
every issue attempted so far. Cycle scale (≤ 15 issues) makes the cost
irrelevant.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any


def runs_dir() -> Path:
    """Return the directory where per-cycle run logs are written.

    Resolved on every call (not module-import time) so tests can redirect
    via ``monkeypatch.setenv("HOME", ...)`` after the module is imported.
    """
    return Path.home() / ".drain-cycle" / "runs"


@dataclass
class RunLog:
    """Per-cycle run-log file. Construct once at orchestrator start.

    Writing the initial ``{cycle_id, cycle_duration_seconds: 0.0,
    entries: []}`` shell in ``__post_init__`` means that even a zero-issue
    cycle (or a crash before the first ``append_entry``) leaves the
    artefact on disk, which is what US-D / kill-condition tooling needs
    to distinguish "drained nothing" from "never ran".

    The filename embeds a UTC run-start timestamp with microsecond
    resolution, so two ``RunLog`` instances on the same ``cycle_id``
    (re-running ``drain-cycle`` after a halt) write to two separate
    files instead of one clobbering the other (ABA-230).
    """

    cycle_id: str
    path: Path = field(init=False)
    entries: list[dict[str, Any]] = field(default_factory=list, init=False)

    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
        directory = runs_dir()
        directory.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        run_started_at = datetime.now(tz=timezone.utc)
        timestamp = run_started_at.strftime("%Y%m%dT%H%M%S%fZ")
        self.path = directory / f"{self.cycle_id}-{timestamp}.json"
        self._persist()

    def append_entry(
        self,
        *,
        issue_identifier: str,
        started_at: str,
        finished_at: str,
        exit_code: int,
        final_linear_state: str,
        worktree_path: str,
        halt_reason: str | None = None,
    ) -> None:
        self.entries.append(
            {
                "issue_identifier": issue_identifier,
                "started_at": started_at,
                "finished_at": finished_at,
                "exit_code": exit_code,
                "final_linear_state": final_linear_state,
                "worktree_path": worktree_path,
                "halt_reason": halt_reason,
            }
        )
        self._persist()

    def _persist(self) -> None:
        payload = {
            "cycle_id": self.cycle_id,
            "cycle_duration_seconds": self._cycle_duration_seconds(),
            "entries": self.entries,
        }
        self.path.write_text(json.dumps(payload, indent=2) + "\n")

    def _cycle_duration_seconds(self) -> float:
        if not self.entries:
            return 0.0
        starts = [datetime.fromisoformat(e["started_at"]) for e in self.entries]
        finishes = [datetime.fromisoformat(e["finished_at"]) for e in self.entries]
        return (max(finishes) - min(starts)).total_seconds()

1	# Design decisions
2	
3	Design rationale for `drain-cycle`. Read this before making architectural changes — `AGENTS.md` points here.
4	
5	Four decisions are recorded so a future reader doesn't have to reverse-engineer them from the code. ADRs would be heavier than this tool needs.
6	
7	## 1. The spawned agent updates Linear itself
8	
9	The orchestrator does **not** poll Linear and write status. The spawned `claude -p` session is told, in its prompt, to move its issue to Done on completion. The orchestrator only reads Linear after the session exits, to decide whether to advance or halt.
10	
11	**Alternative considered.** Orchestrator-owned status: the parent polls Linear, transitions states, owns the lifecycle. This is more conventional and easier to reason about.
12	
13	**Why the agent-self-update path.** The orchestrator can only observe *process exit*, not *task success*. A Claude session may exit 0 having done nothing useful, or exit non-zero having actually shipped — exit code is a poor proxy for "the issue is Done." Letting the agent assert Done in Linear forces it to make an explicit, observable claim about its own outcome, which is exactly the artefact we need to grade KR1 and trigger the kill condition. If this pattern proves unreliable, that's the initiative's kill condition firing — not a bug to paper over.
14	
15	## 2. `--dangerously-skip-permissions` is accepted
16	
17	Every spawned session runs with `--dangerously-skip-permissions`. The agent can run any tool on any path inside its worktree, including shell commands, file writes, and network calls, with no operator approval.
18	
19	**Blast radius.** Bounded to the per-issue worktree under `.worktrees/<issue-identifier>/` inside the target repo, plus the operator's Linear account (the agent can transition issues), plus whatever the spawned shell can reach (env vars, secrets in `~/.config`, network egress). Not bounded to the worktree at the filesystem level — a determined or confused agent can `cd` out.
20	
21	**Why accepted.** The single-operator personal-product context: target repos are mine, the Linear workspace is mine, the machine is mine. The point of the tool is removing prompts; gating them defeats the purpose. The mitigation is **scope discipline at cycle planning** — don't drain a cycle whose issues touch credentials, production systems, or shared infrastructure. This is operator responsibility, not a tool guarantee.
22	
23	## 3. Fresh worktree per issue, not a shared workspace
24	
25	Each issue gets `.worktrees/<issue-identifier>/` branched off `main`, used once, then either removed (on Done) or preserved (on halt).
26	
27	**Alternative considered.** Shared workspace where all issues run in the target repo's main checkout in sequence. Simpler, faster, no worktree plumbing.
28	
29	**Why worktree-per-issue.** Issues drift. An agent that misunderstands its task can leave the workspace in a broken state — half-applied edits, uncommitted files, branch in the wrong place — that contaminates every subsequent issue's starting point. The worktree gives each issue a clean, identical starting point regardless of what the previous one did, and preservation-on-halt (US-B) means inspectable debug state. The cost is filesystem space and a few seconds of branch setup per issue. Cheap.
30	
31	## 4. Run-log is one file per invocation, not one file per cycle
32	
33	Each `drain-cycle` invocation writes its own run-log file at `~/.drain-cycle/runs/<cycle-id>-<run-timestamp>.json`. The per-file schema is unchanged — `{cycle_id, cycle_duration_seconds, entries: [...]}` — and the `cycle_id` carried inside each file is how downstream readers group runs of the same cycle together.
34	
35	**Alternatives considered.** (A) Multi-run schema in one file: `{cycle_id, runs: [...]}`. Faithful, but every reader has to learn the new shape and the on-disk backup file needs migrating. (B) Open-and-extend: load the existing file and append to a single flat `entries` list. Loses run boundaries, and `cycle_duration_seconds` (computed as `max(finished_at) - min(started_at)`) spans the inter-run gap and becomes misleading. (D) Refuse to clobber: fail-fast if the file exists. Breaks the unattended re-run flow the tool exists for (fix X, re-run, drain the rest) until a resume mode is built.
36	
37	**Why per-run files.** The bug being fixed (ABA-230) is that a second invocation against the same cycle silently overwrites the first run's data. Per-run files make the write path single-writer-write-once — no read-then-write race, no schema diff, no migration. US-D (ABA-197), which already plans to glob `runs/*.json` and merge across cycles, gets a one-line addition (group by `cycle_id`) instead of a new shape to read. Each file's `cycle_duration_seconds` represents one invocation's hands-off time; US-D sums them when reporting cycle-level KR2.
38	
39	**Cost.** A re-run cycle accumulates one file per invocation. At cycle scale (≤ 15 issues, rarely more than 2–3 invocations to drain) this is negligible. No retention policy is shipped; trim by hand if it ever matters.
40	
41	## Out of scope (deliberate)
42	
43	- **Parallelism.** Issues run one at a time. The Linear cycle is the unit; intra-cycle parallelism adds resource contention and serialises poorly with the agent-self-update pattern (two agents racing to mark different issues Done is fine, but two agents racing on overlapping files is not).
44	- **Retry.** A halted issue is not retried automatically. The operator inspects the worktree and decides — fix, redo manually, or descope.
45	- **Cross-cycle scheduling.** One cycle per invocation. Chaining cycles is an operator concern.
46

# Design decisions

Design rationale for `drain-cycle`. Read this before making architectural changes — `AGENTS.md` points here.

Four decisions are recorded so a future reader doesn't have to reverse-engineer them from the code. ADRs would be heavier than this tool needs.

## 1. The spawned agent updates Linear itself

The orchestrator does **not** poll Linear and write status. The spawned `claude -p` session is told, in its prompt, to move its issue to Done on completion. The orchestrator only reads Linear after the session exits, to decide whether to advance or halt.

**Alternative considered.** Orchestrator-owned status: the parent polls Linear, transitions states, owns the lifecycle. This is more conventional and easier to reason about.

**Why the agent-self-update path.** The orchestrator can only observe *process exit*, not *task success*. A Claude session may exit 0 having done nothing useful, or exit non-zero having actually shipped — exit code is a poor proxy for "the issue is Done." Letting the agent assert Done in Linear forces it to make an explicit, observable claim about its own outcome, which is exactly the artefact we need to grade KR1 and trigger the kill condition. If this pattern proves unreliable, that's the initiative's kill condition firing — not a bug to paper over.

## 2. `--dangerously-skip-permissions` is accepted

Every spawned session runs with `--dangerously-skip-permissions`. The agent can run any tool on any path inside its worktree, including shell commands, file writes, and network calls, with no operator approval.

**Blast radius.** Bounded to the per-issue worktree under `.worktrees/<issue-identifier>/` inside the target repo, plus the operator's Linear account (the agent can transition issues), plus whatever the spawned shell can reach (env vars, secrets in `~/.config`, network egress). Not bounded to the worktree at the filesystem level — a determined or confused agent can `cd` out.

**Why accepted.** The single-operator personal-product context: target repos are mine, the Linear workspace is mine, the machine is mine. The point of the tool is removing prompts; gating them defeats the purpose. The mitigation is **scope discipline at cycle planning** — don't drain a cycle whose issues touch credentials, production systems, or shared infrastructure. This is operator responsibility, not a tool guarantee.

## 3. Fresh worktree per issue, not a shared workspace

Each issue gets `.worktrees/<issue-identifier>/` branched off `main`, used once, then either removed (on Done) or preserved (on halt).

**Alternative considered.** Shared workspace where all issues run in the target repo's main checkout in sequence. Simpler, faster, no worktree plumbing.

**Why worktree-per-issue.** Issues drift. An agent that misunderstands its task can leave the workspace in a broken state — half-applied edits, uncommitted files, branch in the wrong place — that contaminates every subsequent issue's starting point. The worktree gives each issue a clean, identical starting point regardless of what the previous one did, and preservation-on-halt (US-B) means inspectable debug state. The cost is filesystem space and a few seconds of branch setup per issue. Cheap.

## 4. Run-log is one file per invocation, not one file per cycle

Each `drain-cycle` invocation writes its own run-log file at `~/.drain-cycle/runs/<cycle-id>-<run-timestamp>.json`. The per-file schema is unchanged — `{cycle_id, cycle_duration_seconds, entries: [...]}` — and the `cycle_id` carried inside each file is how downstream readers group runs of the same cycle together.

**Alternatives considered.** (A) Multi-run schema in one file: `{cycle_id, runs: [...]}`. Faithful, but every reader has to learn the new shape and the on-disk backup file needs migrating. (B) Open-and-extend: load the existing file and append to a single flat `entries` list. Loses run boundaries, and `cycle_duration_seconds` (computed as `max(finished_at) - min(started_at)`) spans the inter-run gap and becomes misleading. (D) Refuse to clobber: fail-fast if the file exists. Breaks the unattended re-run flow the tool exists for (fix X, re-run, drain the rest) until a resume mode is built.

**Why per-run files.** The bug being fixed (ABA-230) is that a second invocation against the same cycle silently overwrites the first run's data. Per-run files make the write path single-writer-write-once — no read-then-write race, no schema diff, no migration. US-D (ABA-197), which already plans to glob `runs/*.json` and merge across cycles, gets a one-line addition (group by `cycle_id`) instead of a new shape to read. Each file's `cycle_duration_seconds` represents one invocation's hands-off time; US-D sums them when reporting cycle-level KR2.

**Cost.** A re-run cycle accumulates one file per invocation. At cycle scale (≤ 15 issues, rarely more than 2–3 invocations to drain) this is negligible. No retention policy is shipped; trim by hand if it ever matters.

## Out of scope (deliberate)

- **Parallelism.** Issues run one at a time. The Linear cycle is the unit; intra-cycle parallelism adds resource contention and serialises poorly with the agent-self-update pattern (two agents racing to mark different issues Done is fine, but two agents racing on overlapping files is not).
- **Retry.** A halted issue is not retried automatically. The operator inspects the worktree and decides — fix, redo manually, or descope.
- **Cross-cycle scheduling.** One cycle per invocation. Chaining cycles is an operator concern.

content.content:
1	# drain-cycle
2	
3	Unattended execution of a Linear cycle. One invocation, no prompts; each issue runs in an isolated worktree under a fresh `claude -p` session that updates Linear itself.
4	
5	## Why this exists
6	
7	Today, executing a Linear cycle is manual: launch Claude per issue, watch it run, approve permissions, update Linear, repeat. The blast radius is documented in [`docs/design-decisions.md`](docs/design-decisions.md). If that makes you uncomfortable, that's the right instinct — stick with manual `claude` runs until it doesn't. Comfort with unattended execution is earned, not assumed.
15	- **Personal product, single operator.** One operator, one machine, one Linear workspace. Not designed for shared infra, production-touching cycles, or team workflows.
16

20	- **`git` CLI** on `$PATH`.
21	- **`claude` CLI** on `$PATH` — see the [Claude Code install docs](https://docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/setup).
22	- **Linear API key** with read/write on your Personal team. Generate one at <https://linear.app/settings/api>.

28	cd drain-cycle
29	mise install                          # picks up mise.toml → Python 3.12
30	pip install -e .                      # installs the `drain-cycle` CLI
31	echo 'LINEAR_API_KEY=lin_api_…' > .env  # loaded automatically at CLI start
32	```
33	
34	`.env` lives at the drain-cycle repo root and is gitignored. If you'd rather export `LINEAR_API_KEY` in your shell rc, that still works and takes precedence over `.env`.
35	
36	Verify the install:
37	
38	```bash
39	drain-cycle --help     # any invocation that doesn't crash on import works
40	```
41	
42	## Usage
43	
44	```
45	cd /path/to/target-repo
46	drain-cycle
47	```
48	
49	Drains the current cycle's Todo/Backlog issues (in priority order) until either the cycle is empty (exit 0) or an issue halts (exit 1).
50	
51	### What a run looks like
52	
53	```bash
54	$ cd ~/src/my-project
55	$ drain-cycle
56	
57	# drain-cycle reads the current Linear cycle, fetches its Todo/Backlog issues,
58	# and works through them one at a time. The first issue it picks up:
59	
60	drain-cycle: picked ABA-241: Persist halt_reason in run-log entries
61	
62	# It creates a fresh git worktree under .worktrees/ABA-241/ branched off main,
63	# flips the Linear issue to In Progress, then spawns a `claude -p` session
64	# inside the worktree. The Claude session's own output streams to your
65	# terminal here — same as if you ran it by hand.
66	
67	  (… claude -p session does the work: reads the issue, edits files,
68	       runs tests, commits, pushes, marks the issue Done in Linear …)
69	
70	# When the session exits, drain-cycle re-reads Linear. The issue is now in
71	# the Done column — so the worktree gets cleaned up and the next issue starts.
72	
73	drain-cycle: ABA-241 done; worktree removed.
74	drain-cycle: picked ABA-242: Spec-shaped halt message on stderr
75	
76	  (… second session runs …)
77	
78	drain-cycle: ABA-242 done; worktree removed.
79	drain-cycle: picked ABA-243: Compute cycle_duration_seconds
80	
81	  (… third session runs, but doesn't finish the work …)
82	
83	# The session exited but the Linear issue is still In Progress — meaning the
84	# agent didn't mark it Done. drain-cycle stops the run immediately so you can
85	# investigate. The worktree is left in place; the run log captures the same
86	# halt line you see on screen.
87	
88	Halt: ABA-243 (final state: In Progress) at /Users/you/src/my-project/.worktrees/ABA-243
89	$ echo $?
90	1
91	```
92	
93	After the run, the per-cycle JSON log is at `~/.drain-cycle/runs/<cycle-id>.json`. On halt, the worktree at `.worktrees/<issue-identifier>/` is preserved for inspection; on success it's removed.

101

103

file.content:
# drain-cycle

Unattended execution of a Linear cycle. One invocation, no prompts; each issue runs in an isolated worktree under a fresh `claude -p` session that updates Linear itself.

## Why this exists

Today, executing a Linear cycle is manual: launch Claude per issue, watch it run, approve permissions, update Linear, repeat. Execution shepherding consumes time that should go to *scoping the next cycle* and *validating delivered work*. `drain-cycle` removes that shepherding so attention shifts back to scoping and validation. The full goal / KRs / kill condition live in the Linear project description.

## Is this for you?

Read this section before installing — `drain-cycle` is deliberately not for everyone.

- **You need to be confident in your cycle planning.** `drain-cycle` executes the cycle you scoped — it does not reshape it. A poorly-scoped cycle drains faster than a manually-shepherded one, but the result is poorly-scoped work shipped faster. Plan deliberately; this tool is the multiplier, not the safety net.
- **Accept the risk profile.** Every spawned session runs with `--dangerously-skip-permissions`. The agent can write files, run shell commands, hit the network, and update Linear without asking. The blast radius is documented in [`docs/design-decisions.md`](docs/design-decisions.md). If that makes you uncomfortable, that's the right instinct — stick with manual `claude` runs until it doesn't. Comfort with unattended execution is earned, not assumed.
- **Personal product, single operator.** One operator, one machine, one Linear workspace. Not designed for shared infra, production-touching cycles, or team workflows.

## Prerequisites

- **Python 3.11+** — pinned to 3.12 via `mise.toml`. Install [`mise`](https://mise.jdx.dev/) first.
- **`git` CLI** on `$PATH`.
- **`claude` CLI** on `$PATH` — see the [Claude Code install docs](https://docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/setup).
- **Linear API key** with read/write on your Personal team. Generate one at <https://linear.app/settings/api>.

## Install

```bash
git clone https://github.com/ababushkin/drain-cycle
cd drain-cycle
mise install                          # picks up mise.toml → Python 3.12
pip install -e .                      # installs the `drain-cycle` CLI
echo 'LINEAR_API_KEY=lin_api_…' > .env  # loaded automatically at CLI start
```

`.env` lives at the drain-cycle repo root and is gitignored. If you'd rather export `LINEAR_API_KEY` in your shell rc, that still works and takes precedence over `.env`.

Verify the install:

```bash
drain-cycle --help     # any invocation that doesn't crash on import works
```

## Usage

```
cd /path/to/target-repo
drain-cycle
```

Drains the current cycle's Todo/Backlog issues (in priority order) until either the cycle is empty (exit 0) or an issue halts (exit 1).

### What a run looks like

```bash
$ cd ~/src/my-project
$ drain-cycle

# drain-cycle reads the current Linear cycle, fetches its Todo/Backlog issues,
# and works through them one at a time. The first issue it picks up:

drain-cycle: picked ABA-241: Persist halt_reason in run-log entries

# It creates a fresh git worktree under .worktrees/ABA-241/ branched off main,
# flips the Linear issue to In Progress, then spawns a `claude -p` session
# inside the worktree. The Claude session's own output streams to your
# terminal here — same as if you ran it by hand.

  (… claude -p session does the work: reads the issue, edits files,
       runs tests, commits, pushes, marks the issue Done in Linear …)

# When the session exits, drain-cycle re-reads Linear. The issue is now in
# the Done column — so the worktree gets cleaned up and the next issue starts.

drain-cycle: ABA-241 done; worktree removed.
drain-cycle: picked ABA-242: Spec-shaped halt message on stderr

  (… second session runs …)

drain-cycle: ABA-242 done; worktree removed.
drain-cycle: picked ABA-243: Compute cycle_duration_seconds

  (… third session runs, but doesn't finish the work …)

# The session exited but the Linear issue is still In Progress — meaning the
# agent didn't mark it Done. drain-cycle stops the run immediately so you can
# investigate. The worktree is left in place; the run log captures the same
# halt line you see on screen.

Halt: ABA-243 (final state: In Progress) at /Users/you/src/my-project/.worktrees/ABA-243
$ echo $?
1
```

After the run, the per-cycle JSON log is at `~/.drain-cycle/runs/<cycle-id>.json`. On halt, the worktree at `.worktrees/<issue-identifier>/` is preserved for inspection; on success it's removed.

## Recommended companion skills

`drain-cycle` assumes the spawned `claude -p` sessions have access to whatever skills you've installed globally. The pairing it was designed for:

- [**`ababushkin/pde-skills`**](https://github.com/ababushkin/pde-skills) — planning + engineering skill pack. Use it to *shape* the cycle (initiative, KRs, slices) before draining it. `drain-cycle` only multiplies execution; the quality of the cycle is upstream of this tool.
- [**`addyosmani/agent-skills`**](https://github.com/addyosmani/agent-skills) — Addy Osmani's pack covers complementary build / test / review skills the spawned sessions lean on.

Both are skill packs for Claude Code — install them globally and the spawned `claude -p` sessions will pick them up automatically.

## Logs & grading

Every invocation writes one JSON file: `~/.drain-cycle/runs/<cycle-id>.json`. One entry per attempted issue, including timestamps, exit code, final Linear state, worktree path, and `halt_reason` on the halting entry. Use it to gauge how cleanly your runs complete — see [`drain_cycle/runlog.py`](drain_cycle/runlog.py) for the schema.

## Design

Design decisions, alternatives considered, and deliberate out-of-scope choices live in [`docs/design-decisions.md`](docs/design-decisions.md).

4	``grade`` → grade.run, ``-h`` / ``--help`` → usage + exit 0, anything
5	else → usage on stderr + exit 2. The behaviour guarded against is the
6	old fall-through where a misspelled subcommand silently triggered a
7	real cycle drain.
8	"""
9	from __future__ import annotations
10	
11	import pytest
12	
13	from drain_cycle import cli, grade, orchestrator

17	    called: list[bool] = []
18	
19	    def fake_run() -> int:
20	        called.append(True)
21	        return 0
22	
23	    monkeypatch.setattr(orchestrator, "run", fake_run)
24	    monkeypatch.setattr(cli, "load_dotenv", lambda *_a, **_kw: False)
25	    return called

27

37	    return called
38	
39

41	    called = _stub_no_op_orchestrator(monkeypatch)
42	    monkeypatch.setattr("sys.argv", ["drain-cycle"])
43	
44	    with pytest.raises(SystemExit) as exc:
45	        cli.main()
46	
47	    assert exc.value.code == 0
48	    assert called == [True]

50

52	    monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch,
53	) -> None:

57	    with pytest.raises(SystemExit) as exc:

"""CLI dispatch tests for ``drain-cycle``.

Pins the four dispatch paths in ``cli.main``: zero-arg → orchestrator,
``grade`` → grade.run, ``-h`` / ``--help`` → usage + exit 0, anything
else → usage on stderr + exit 2. The behaviour guarded against is the
old fall-through where a misspelled subcommand silently triggered a
real cycle drain.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import pytest

from drain_cycle import cli, grade, orchestrator


def _stub_no_op_orchestrator(monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch) -> list[bool]:
    called: list[bool] = []

    def fake_run() -> int:
        called.append(True)
        return 0

    monkeypatch.setattr(orchestrator, "run", fake_run)
    monkeypatch.setattr(cli, "load_dotenv", lambda *_a, **_kw: False)
    return called


def _stub_no_op_grade(monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch) -> list[bool]:
    called: list[bool] = []

    def fake_run(runs_dir) -> int:
        called.append(True)
        return 0

    monkeypatch.setattr(grade, "run", fake_run)
    monkeypatch.setattr(cli, "load_dotenv", lambda *_a, **_kw: False)
    return called


def test_no_args_dispatches_to_orchestrator(monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch) -> None:
    called = _stub_no_op_orchestrator(monkeypatch)
    monkeypatch.setattr("sys.argv", ["drain-cycle"])

    with pytest.raises(SystemExit) as exc:
        cli.main()

    assert exc.value.code == 0
    assert called == [True]


def test_grade_subcommand_dispatches_to_grade_run(
    monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch,
) -> None:
    called = _stub_no_op_grade(monkeypatch)
    monkeypatch.setattr("sys.argv", ["drain-cycle", "grade"])

    with pytest.raises(SystemExit) as exc:
        cli.main()

    assert exc.value.code == 0
    assert called == [True]


@pytest.mark.parametrize("flag", ["-h", "--help"])
def test_help_flag_prints_usage_and_exits_zero(
    monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch,
    capsys: pytest.CaptureFixture[str],
    flag: str,
) -> None:
    monkeypatch.setattr(cli, "load_dotenv", lambda *_a, **_kw: False)
    monkeypatch.setattr("sys.argv", ["drain-cycle", flag])

    with pytest.raises(SystemExit) as exc:
        cli.main()

    assert exc.value.code == 0
    captured = capsys.readouterr()
    assert "usage:" in captured.out
    assert "drain the current Linear cycle" in captured.out


@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "argv_tail",
    [
        ["bogus"],
        ["grade", "extra"],
        ["grade", "--verbose"],
        ["--unknown"],
    ],
)
def test_unknown_invocation_prints_usage_to_stderr_and_exits_two(
    monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch,
    capsys: pytest.CaptureFixture[str],
    argv_tail: list[str],
) -> None:
    """The old fall-through silently ran the orchestrator on `drain-cycle bogus`.
    That's a foot-gun — the orchestrator is destructive (creates worktrees,
    transitions Linear state). Misspellings must fail loudly."""
    forbid_orchestrator(monkeypatch)
    forbid_grade(monkeypatch)
    monkeypatch.setattr(cli, "load_dotenv", lambda *_a, **_kw: False)
    monkeypatch.setattr("sys.argv", ["drain-cycle", *argv_tail])

    with pytest.raises(SystemExit) as exc:
        cli.main()

    assert exc.value.code == 2
    captured = capsys.readouterr()
    assert "unknown invocation" in captured.err
    assert "usage:" in captured.err


def forbid_orchestrator(monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch) -> None:
    def boom() -> int:
        raise AssertionError("orchestrator.run() must not be called on unknown args")

    monkeypatch.setattr(orchestrator, "run", boom)


def forbid_grade(monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch) -> None:
    def boom(_runs_dir) -> int:
        raise AssertionError("grade.run() must not be called on unknown args")

    monkeypatch.setattr(grade, "run", boom)

content.content:
1	"""Halt-on-not-Done test for the orchestrator (Task 5 / ABA-202).
2	
3	Task 3 (ABA-200) added the iteration loop and a minimal non-Done branch that
4	returns ``1`` without removing the worktree. This test pins that contract:
5	if the spawned session exits without flipping the issue to Done, the
6	orchestrator must (a) exit non-zero, (b) leave the worktree on disk so the
7	operator can inspect it, and (c) not touch any subsequent issue.
8	
9	The second test in this file (Task 2 / ABA-216) pins the halt-path slice
10	of US-C: the run-log artefact must contain exactly one entry for the
11	halted issue, with `final_linear_state` reflecting the non-Done state
12	the agent left it in — so KR1 grading sees a halted attempt rather than
13	a missing one.
14	
15	Substitution choices mirror ``test_orchestrator_iteration.py``: real git
16	repo, in-process Linear stub via attribute monkey-patching, fake ``claude``
17	shell script as ``_CLAUDE_CMD``. The difference is the script: here it
18	exits without writing the marker file, so the stubbed ``get_issue`` keeps
19	returning the original Todo state — which is exactly the production
20	failure mode this test guards against.
21	"""
22	from __future__ import annotations
23	
24	import json
25	import subprocess
26	from datetime import datetime
27	from pathlib import Path
28	
29	import pytest
30	
31	from drain_cycle import linear, orchestrator
32	
33	
34	def _issue(identifier: str, priority: int, sort_order: float) -> dict:
35	    return {
36	        "id": f"id-{identifier}",
37	        "identifier": identifier,
38	        "title": f"Title for {identifier}",
39	        "description": f"Body for {identifier}",
40	        "priority": priority,
41	        "sortOrder": sort_order,
42	        "state": {"type": "unstarted", "name": "Todo"},
43	    }
44	
45	
46	def _init_repo(repo: Path) -> None:
47	    subprocess.run(["git", "init", "-b", "main"], cwd=repo, check=True, capture_output=True)
48	    subprocess.run(
49	        ["git", "config", "user.email", "test@example.com"], cwd=repo, check=True
50	    )
51	    subprocess.run(["git", "config", "user.name", "Test"], cwd=repo, check=True)
52	    (repo / "README.md").write_text("seed\n")
53	    subprocess.run(["git", "add", "."], cwd=repo, check=True, capture_output=True)
54	    subprocess.run(
55	        ["git", "commit", "-m", "seed"], cwd=repo, check=True, capture_output=True
56	    )
57	
58	
59	def test_orchestrator_halts_when_spawn_leaves_issue_not_done(
60	    tmp_path: Path, monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch, capsys: pytest.CaptureFixture[str]
61	) -> None:
62	    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
63	    repo.mkdir()
64	    _init_repo(repo)
65	    monkeypatch.chdir(repo)
66	    monkeypatch.setenv("HOME", str(tmp_path))
67	
68	    # Two issues; the first will be picked, the spawn will exit without
69	    # marking it Done. The second must remain untouched.
70	    first = _issue("ABA-FIRST", priority=1, sort_order=1.0)
71	    second = _issue("ABA-SECOND", priority=2, sort_order=2.0)
72	    raw_issues = [first, second]
73	    issues_by_id = {i["id"]: i for i in raw_issues}
74	
75	    get_issue_calls: list[str] = []
76	
77	    def fake_current_cycle_id() -> str:
78	        return "stub-cycle"
79	
80	    def fake_pending_issues(cycle_id: str) -> list[dict]:
81	        return linear._sort_pending_issues(raw_issues)
82	
83	    def fake_get_issue(issue_id: str) -> dict:
84	        # The fake claude script never flips state, so this always returns
85	        # the original Todo issue. Recording the calls lets us prove the
86	        # orchestrator stopped after the first re-fetch.
87	        get_issue_calls.append(issue_id)
88	        return issues_by_id[issue_id]
89	
90	    monkeypatch.setattr(linear, "current_cycle_id", fake_current_cycle_id)
91	    monkeypatch.setattr(linear, "pending_issues", fake_pending_issues)
92	    monkeypatch.setattr(linear, "get_issue", fake_get_issue)
93	    # set_state is exercised by tests/test_orchestrator_set_state.py; here it's
94	    # a no-op so this test stays focused on halt behaviour.
95	    monkeypatch.setattr(linear, "set_state", lambda issue_id, state_name: None)
96	
97	    fake_claude = _write_fake_claude_script(tmp_path)
98	    monkeypatch.setattr(orchestrator, "_CLAUDE_CMD", [str(fake_claude)])
99	
100	    exit_code = orchestrator.run()
101	
102	    assert exit_code != 0
103	    # Loop bailed before touching the second issue. The first id may be
104	    # re-fetched more than once (initial refresh + post-revert refresh per
105	    # ABA-229); the load-bearing invariant is the second issue is absent.
106	    assert get_issue_calls
107	    assert second["id"] not in get_issue_calls
108	    # Second issue's stub state is unchanged.
109	    assert second["state"] == {"type": "unstarted", "name": "Todo"}
110	    # First issue's worktree is still on disk for the operator to inspect.
111	    first_worktree = repo / ".worktrees" / first["identifier"]
112	    assert first_worktree.is_dir()
113	    # Second issue's worktree was never created.
114	    second_worktree = repo / ".worktrees" / second["identifier"]
115	    assert not second_worktree.exists()
116	
117	    # Spec'd halt UX (US-B / ABA-212): a single line on stderr starting with
118	    # `Halt: ` carrying issue identifier, final state name, and the absolute
119	    # worktree path the operator must `cd` to. The `Halt:` token is the
120	    # unique grep anchor — no other stderr line on either branch starts
121	    # with it.
122	    stderr_lines = capsys.readouterr().err.splitlines()
123	    halt_lines = [line for line in stderr_lines if line.startswith("Halt: ")]
124	    assert len(halt_lines) == 1
125	    (halt_line,) = halt_lines
126	    assert first["identifier"] in halt_line
127	    assert first["state"]["name"] in halt_line
128	    assert str(first_worktree) in halt_line

131	def _write_fake_claude_script(tmp_path: Path) -> Path:
132	    """No-op stand-in for ``claude -p`` that exits cleanly without doing work.
133	
134	    The point of the test is the orchestrator's response when a spawn
135	    *succeeds at the process level* but leaves the issue in its original
136	    state — the most pernicious failure mode, because there is no
137	    non-zero exit code to alert on.
138	    """
139	    script = tmp_path / "fake-claude.sh"
140	    script.write_text("#!/bin/sh\nexit 0\n")
141	    script.chmod(0o755)
142	    return script

147	) -> None:
148	    """Halt-path slice of US-C (Task 2 / ABA-216).
149	
150	    The run-log file must contain exactly one entry — for the halted
151	    issue — with `final_linear_state` matching the non-Done state name
152	    the agent left it in. The second issue must be absent from entries
153	    (never attempted) and absent from disk (worktree never created).

164	    second = _issue("ABA-SECOND", priority=2, sort_order=2.0)
165	    raw_issues = [first, second]
166	    issues_by_id = {i["id"]: i for i in raw_issues}
167	
168	    def fake_current_cycle_id() -> str:
169	        return "stub-cycle-id"
170	
171	    def fake_pending_issues(cycle_id: str) -> list[dict]:
172	        return linear._sort_pending_issues(raw_issues)

174	    def fake_get_issue(issue_id: str) -> dict:
175	        return issues_by_id[issue_id]

178	    monkeypatch.setattr(linear, "pending_issues", fake_pending_issues)
179	    monkeypatch.setattr(linear, "get_issue", fake_get_issue)
180	    monkeypatch.setattr(linear, "set_state", lambda issue_id, state_name: None)
181	
182	    fake_claude = _write_fake_claude_script(tmp_path)
183	    monkeypatch.setattr(orchestrator, "_CLAUDE_CMD", [str(fake_claude)])
184	
185	    exit_code = orchestrator.run()
186	    assert exit_code != 0
187	
188	    # Per-run filename (ABA-230): one file per drain-cycle invocation,
189	    # ``<cycle-id>-<run-timestamp>.json`` — glob to locate it.
190	    runs_dir = tmp_path / ".drain-cycle" / "runs"
191	    log_files = list(runs_dir.glob("stub-cycle-id-*.json"))
192	    assert len(log_files) == 1
193	    log_path = log_files[0]
194	    payload = json.loads(log_path.read_text())
195	
196	    assert payload["cycle_id"] == "stub-cycle-id"
197	    assert len(payload["entries"]) == 1
198	
199	    # Halt entry's wall-clock duration is the cycle's duration — KR2 still
200	    # accrues the unattended time we spent on the doomed issue.
201	    only_entry = payload["entries"][0]
202	    expected_duration = (
203	        datetime.fromisoformat(only_entry["finished_at"])
204	        - datetime.fromisoformat(only_entry["started_at"])

207	    assert payload["cycle_duration_seconds"] >= 0.0

213	        "finished_at",
214	        "exit_code",
215	        "final_linear_state",
216	        "worktree_path",
217	        "halt_reason",
218	    }
219	    assert set(entry.keys()) == required_keys
220	    assert entry["issue_identifier"] == first["identifier"]
221	    # The agent never flipped state, so the halted entry records "Todo"
222	    # — load-bearing for KR1 grading (must distinguish halt from success).
223	    assert entry["final_linear_state"] == first["state"]["name"]
224	    assert entry["final_linear_state"] != "Done"
225	    assert entry["worktree_path"] == str(repo / ".worktrees" / first["identifier"])

232	    (halt_line,) = [line for line in stderr_lines if line.startswith("Halt: ")]
233	    assert entry["halt_reason"] == halt_line

248	    assert (repo / ".worktrees" / first["identifier"]).is_dir()

251	def test_orchestrator_records_halt_when_setup_raises_before_spawn(
252	    tmp_path: Path, monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch, capsys: pytest.CaptureFixture[str]
253	) -> None:
254	    """A pre-spawn failure (e.g. Linear outage during ``set_state``) must
255	    convert to a recorded halt — run-log entry + Halt: stderr line + exit 1
256	    — instead of an uncaught traceback. Without this, the orchestrator
257	    crashes mid-cycle and the runlog is missing the attempted entry, so
258	    KR1 grading sees a phantom-zero rather than a halt.
259	    """
260	    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
261	    repo.mkdir()
262	    _init_repo(repo)
263	    monkeypatch.chdir(repo)
264	    monkeypatch.setenv("HOME", str(tmp_path))
265	
266	    first = _issue("ABA-FIRST", priority=1, sort_order=1.0)
267	    second = _issue("ABA-SECOND", priority=2, sort_order=2.0)
268	    raw_issues = [first, second]
269	
270	    def fake_current_cycle_id() -> str:
271	        return "stub-cycle-id"
272	
273	    def fake_pending_issues(cycle_id: str) -> list[dict]:
274	        return linear._sort_pending_issues(raw_issues)
275	
276	    def failing_set_state(issue_id: str, state_name: str) -> None:
277	        raise RuntimeError("Linear outage simulated")
278	
279	    # get_issue must never be called — the orchestrator should halt before
280	    # the spawn, so there's no post-spawn state refresh.
281	    def forbidden_get_issue(issue_id: str) -> dict:
282	        raise AssertionError("get_issue called after setup failure")
283	
284	    monkeypatch.setattr(linear, "current_cycle_id", fake_current_cycle_id)
285	    monkeypatch.setattr(linear, "pending_issues", fake_pending_issues)
286	    monkeypatch.setattr(linear, "set_state", failing_set_state)
287	    monkeypatch.setattr(linear, "get_issue", forbidden_get_issue)
288	
289	    # Claude must never be spawned either — the failure is before spawn.
290	    forbidden_claude = tmp_path / "forbidden-claude.sh"
291	    forbidden_claude.write_text("#!/bin/sh\nexit 99\n")
292	    forbidden_claude.chmod(0o755)
293	    monkeypatch.setattr(orchestrator, "_CLAUDE_CMD", [str(forbidden_claude)])
294	
295	    exit_code = orchestrator.run()
296	    assert exit_code == 1
297	
298	    runs_dir = tmp_path / ".drain-cycle" / "runs"
299	    log_files = list(runs_dir.glob("stub-cycle-id-*.json"))
300	    assert len(log_files) == 1
301	    payload = json.loads(log_files[0].read_text())
302	
303	    # One entry recorded for the failing-setup issue; second issue absent.
304	    assert len(payload["entries"]) == 1
305	    entry = payload["entries"][0]
306	    assert entry["issue_identifier"] == first["identifier"]
307	    # Final Linear state is the pre-failure state — set_state never landed.
308	    assert entry["final_linear_state"] == first["state"]["name"]
309	    # halt_reason carries the same string emitted to stderr (US-B / ABA-213).
310	    stderr_lines = capsys.readouterr().err.splitlines()
311	    (halt_line,) = [line for line in stderr_lines if line.startswith("Halt: ")]
312	    assert entry["halt_reason"] == halt_line
313	    # The halt message surfaces the underlying exception so the operator
314	    # can diagnose without re-reading a stack trace.
315	    assert "Linear outage simulated" in halt_line
316	    assert "setup failed" in halt_line
317	
318	    # Second issue never attempted — same contract as a spawn-time halt.
319	    assert all(
320	        e["issue_identifier"] != second["identifier"] for e in payload["entries"]
321	    )

391	def test_orchestrator_reverts_state_on_timeout_halt(
392	    tmp_path: Path, monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch, capsys: pytest.CaptureFixture[str]
393	) -> None:
394	    """ABA-229 AC1: on spawn-timeout halt, the orchestrator must revert the
395	    issue's state back to the pre-halt state name captured from the cycle
396	    query — so a re-run picks it up again rather than silently skipping
397	    it (the ABA-227 silent-skip case).
398	    """
399	    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
400	    repo.mkdir()
401	    _init_repo(repo)
402	    monkeypatch.chdir(repo)
403	    monkeypatch.setenv("HOME", str(tmp_path))
404	
405	    first = _issue("ABA-FIRST", priority=1, sort_order=1.0)
406	    raw_issues = [first]
407	    issues_by_id = {i["id"]: i for i in raw_issues}
408	    set_state_calls: list[tuple[str, str]] = []
409	    # Tracks Linear-side state per issue so a successful revert is visible
410	    # on the post-revert refresh.
411	    live_states: dict[str, dict[str, str]] = {
412	        first["id"]: first["state"].copy(),
413	    }
414	
415	    def fake_set_state(issue_id: str, state_name: str) -> None:
416	        set_state_calls.append((issue_id, state_name))
417	        live_states[issue_id] = {
418	            "type": "started" if state_name == "In Progress" else "unstarted",
419	            "name": state_name,
420	        }
421	
422	    def fake_get_issue(issue_id: str) -> dict:
423	        return {**issues_by_id[issue_id], "state": live_states[issue_id]}
424	
425	    monkeypatch.setattr(linear, "current_cycle_id", lambda: "stub-cycle-id")
426	    monkeypatch.setattr(
427	        linear, "pending_issues", lambda cycle_id: linear._sort_pending_issues(raw_issues)
428	    )
429	    monkeypatch.setattr(linear, "set_state", fake_set_state)
430	    monkeypatch.setattr(linear, "get_issue", fake_get_issue)
431	
432	    hanging_claude = tmp_path / "hanging-claude.sh"
433	    hanging_claude.write_text("#!/bin/sh\nsleep 10\n")
434	    hanging_claude.chmod(0o755)
435	    monkeypatch.setattr(orchestrator, "_CLAUDE_CMD", [str(hanging_claude)])
436	    monkeypatch.setattr(orchestrator, "_ISSUE_TIMEOUT_SECONDS", 0.3)
437	
438	    exit_code = orchestrator.run()
439	    assert exit_code == 1
440	
441	    # Two set_state calls in pick order: In Progress (pre-spawn), then
442	    # revert to the original "Todo" (post-halt).
443	    assert set_state_calls == [
444	        (first["id"], "In Progress"),
445	        (first["id"], first["state"]["name"]),
446	    ]
447	    # Linear ends in the pre-halt state — what makes a re-run pick it up.
448	    assert live_states[first["id"]]["name"] == first["state"]["name"]
449	
450	    # The halt entry's final_linear_state reflects the post-revert state
451	    # observed via refresh, not the in-flight "In Progress".
452	    runs_dir = tmp_path / ".drain-cycle" / "runs"
453	    payload = json.loads(next(runs_dir.glob("stub-cycle-id-*.json")).read_text())
454	    entry = payload["entries"][0]
455	    assert entry["final_linear_state"] == first["state"]["name"]
456	
457	    # Same string lands on stderr (US-B / ABA-213 invariant).
458	    stderr_lines = capsys.readouterr().err.splitlines()
459	    (halt_line,) = [line for line in stderr_lines if line.startswith("Halt: ")]
460	    assert entry["halt_reason"] == halt_line
461	    assert first["state"]["name"] in halt_line

527	def test_orchestrator_halt_records_revert_failure_non_fatally(
528	    tmp_path: Path, monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch, capsys: pytest.CaptureFixture[str]
529	) -> None:
530	    """ABA-229 AC4: if the revert ``set_state`` call raises, the orchestrator
531	    still exits 1 with a halt entry written. The halt-reason explicitly
532	    notes the revert failure so the operator can fix the state by hand,
533	    and no traceback escapes.
534	    """
535	    repo = tmp_path / "repo"
536	    repo.mkdir()
537	    _init_repo(repo)
538	    monkeypatch.chdir(repo)
539	    monkeypatch.setenv("HOME", str(tmp_path))
540	
541	    first = _issue("ABA-FIRST", priority=1, sort_order=1.0)
542	    raw_issues = [first]
543	    issues_by_id = {i["id"]: i for i in raw_issues}
544	    set_state_calls: list[tuple[str, str]] = []
545	    revert_error_message = "Linear unavailable during revert"
546	
547	    def fake_set_state(issue_id: str, state_name: str) -> None:
548	        set_state_calls.append((issue_id, state_name))
549	        # First call (Todo → In Progress) succeeds; second call (revert)
550	        # raises to simulate a Linear outage at exactly the wrong moment.
551	        if len(set_state_calls) >= 2:
552	            raise RuntimeError(revert_error_message)
553	
554	    def fake_get_issue(issue_id: str) -> dict:
555	        # Agent left the issue in In Progress — the case that motivated
